note: Held-out, exam-style paraphrase items for the spec 7d / PRD AC4 paraphrase test. Each concept links to a base memory card via its concept_id (the base card carries tags 'concept::<id> paraphrase::source'). The build script tags every item here with: <section_tag> concept::<id> perf::paraphrase holdout::performance variant::<n>. These items are reworded to test the SAME principle in NEW words/scenarios so that card recall can be compared against performance on unseen items.

concepts.base_principle: Lys, Arg, His carry positive side-chain charge near physiological pH.

items.stem: A drug must form an ionic bond with a phosphate group on its protein target at blood pH. A side chain of which residue is best suited?
items.rationale: Lysine is positively charged at pH 7.4 and pairs electrostatically with the negative phosphate.

items.stem: At pH 7.4, which residue's side chain carries a net positive charge?
items.rationale: Arginine's guanidinium side chain is protonated and positive at physiological pH.

concepts.base_principle: Nonpolar residues bury in the core via the hydrophobic effect.

items.stem: In a globular cytosolic protein, where are leucine and isoleucine side chains most likely located?

options.D: Forming an ion pair with water

items.rationale: Nonpolar side chains minimize contact with water by packing into the interior.

items.stem: A mutation replaces a buried valine with glutamate. What is the most likely consequence?

options.B: Destabilizes the fold by placing a charge in the nonpolar interior

items.rationale: Introducing a charged residue into the hydrophobic core is energetically unfavorable and destabilizing.

concepts.base_principle: Km is [S] at half Vmax; lower Km means higher apparent affinity.

items.stem: Enzyme X has Km = 2 mM and enzyme Y has Km = 20 mM for the same substrate. Which enzyme has the higher apparent affinity?
items.rationale: A lower Km means half-maximal velocity is reached at lower substrate concentration, i.e., higher apparent affinity.

items.stem: When the substrate concentration equals Km, the reaction velocity equals what fraction of Vmax?
items.rationale: By definition Km is the substrate concentration at half of Vmax.

concepts.base_principle: Competitive inhibitors raise apparent Km; Vmax unchanged.

items.stem: A competitive inhibitor is added, then substrate is raised to a large excess. What happens to Vmax?
items.rationale: Excess substrate outcompetes a competitive inhibitor, so Vmax is recovered.

items.stem: A molecule resembling the substrate reversibly occupies the active site. How is the apparent Km affected?
items.rationale: Competitive inhibition raises the apparent Km because more substrate is needed to reach half Vmax.

concepts.base_principle: DNA strands are antiparallel and complementary.

items.stem: One DNA strand reads 5'-ATGC-3'. Written 5' to 3', the complementary strand is:
items.rationale: The antiparallel complement of 5'-ATGC-3' is 3'-TACG-5', which written 5' to 3' is GCAT.

items.stem: Describing the two strands of a DNA duplex as 'antiparallel' means that:

options.A: Both run 5' to 3' in the same direction

items.rationale: Antiparallel means the strands point in opposite 5'-to-3' directions.

concepts.base_principle: Polymerases synthesize 5' to 3', adding to the 3'-OH.

items.stem: RNA polymerase reads the template strand in which direction and synthesizes RNA in which direction?
items.rationale: Polymerase reads template 3'->5' and builds the new strand 5'->3'.

items.stem: During strand synthesis, a new nucleotide is added to which end of the growing chain?
items.rationale: Nucleotides are added to the free 3'-hydroxyl, giving 5'->3' synthesis.

concepts.base_principle: The genetic code is degenerate (redundant).

items.stem: A single-base substitution in the third codon position often leaves the protein unchanged. The best explanation is that:
items.rationale: Multiple codons encode the same amino acid, so many third-position changes are silent.

items.stem: There are 61 sense codons but only 20 amino acids. This directly implies that:

options.A: Many amino acids are specified by more than one codon
options.B: Each amino acid has exactly one codon
options.D: Each codon encodes two amino acids

items.rationale: More codons than amino acids means redundancy (degeneracy).

concepts.base_principle: Glycolysis nets 2 ATP per glucose.

items.stem: What is the NET ATP yield from glycolysis of one glucose by substrate-level phosphorylation?
items.rationale: Four ATP are produced and two are invested, for a net of two.

items.stem: Glycolysis invests 2 ATP and produces 4 ATP. The net gain is:
items.rationale: 4 produced minus 2 invested equals a net of 2 ATP.

concepts.base_principle: O2 is the final electron acceptor of the ETC.

items.stem: Cyanide blocks complex IV so oxygen can no longer accept electrons. The immediate effect on the electron transport chain is that:

options.B: Electrons back up and the proton gradient collapses

items.rationale: Without the terminal acceptor, the chain cannot pass electrons, the gradient dissipates, and ATP synthesis stops.

items.stem: The terminal electron acceptor of the mitochondrial electron transport chain is:
items.rationale: Oxygen accepts electrons at complex IV and is reduced to water.

concepts.base_principle: Cholesterol buffers fluidity; unsaturation increases it.

items.stem: At low temperature, cholesterol affects a phospholipid membrane by:

options.A: Increasing fluidity by preventing tight packing

items.rationale: Cholesterol prevents fatty-acid tails from packing tightly in the cold, maintaining fluidity.

items.stem: Replacing saturated phospholipid tails with unsaturated (kinked) tails will:
items.rationale: Kinks from double bonds prevent tight packing, raising fluidity.

concepts.base_principle: Na+/K+ ATPase: 3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in per ATP.

items.stem: Per cycle, the Na+/K+ ATPase moves:
items.rationale: It exports 3 sodium and imports 2 potassium ions per ATP hydrolyzed.

items.stem: A toxin halts the Na+/K+ ATPase. Over time the resting membrane potential will:

options.B: Run down toward depolarization as the gradients dissipate

items.rationale: Loss of pumping collapses the ion gradients that maintain the resting potential.

concepts.base_principle: Meiosis yields four haploid, varied cells with recombination.

items.stem: Which process yields four genetically distinct haploid cells?
items.rationale: Meiosis halves ploidy and generates variation, producing four distinct haploid cells.

items.stem: Crossing over and independent assortment occur during:
items.rationale: Both diversity-generating events occur in meiosis (prophase/metaphase I).

concepts.base_principle: Horizontal and vertical motion are independent.

items.stem: A ball is thrown horizontally off a cliff at the same instant another is dropped from the same height. Ignoring air resistance, which lands first?

options.C: They land at the same time
options.D: It depends on the throw speed

items.rationale: Vertical motion is independent of horizontal motion; both have the same vertical drop.

items.stem: For a projectile with no air resistance, the horizontal component of velocity during flight:
items.rationale: No horizontal force acts, so horizontal velocity is constant.

concepts.base_principle: Faster fluid flow corresponds to lower pressure.

items.stem: Blood flows through a narrowed (stenotic) arterial segment. Compared with the wider region, in the narrow region the speed and the pressure are:
items.rationale: Continuity raises speed in the narrow region; Bernoulli then predicts lower pressure there.

items.stem: Air moves faster over the top of an airplane wing than beneath it. The pressure on the top surface is therefore:
items.rationale: Higher speed along a streamline corresponds to lower pressure (Bernoulli).

concepts.base_principle: PV = nRT; P and V inversely related at constant T.

items.stem: At constant temperature, a fixed gas sample's volume is halved. Its pressure:
items.rationale: Boyle's law: P and V are inversely proportional at constant T and n.

items.stem: Per the ideal gas law, doubling the absolute temperature at constant pressure does what to the volume?
items.rationale: At constant P and n, volume is proportional to absolute temperature.

concepts.base_principle: V = IR.

items.stem: A 12 V battery drives 3 A through a single resistor. The resistance is:
Holding the voltage constant, doubling a resistor's resistance changes the current to:
items.rationale: I = V/R, so doubling R halves the current.

concepts.base_principle: Each half-life halves the remaining amount.

items.stem: A radioisotope has a half-life of 8 days. After 24 days, what fraction of the original remains?
items.rationale: 24 days is 3 half-lives; (1/2)^3 = 1/8.

items.stem: Starting with 100 g of an isotope whose half-life is 5 years, how much remains after 10 years?
items.rationale: 10 years is 2 half-lives; 100 -> 50 -> 25 g.

concepts.base_principle: Strong acids fully dissociate; pH = -log[H+].

items.stem: What is the pH of 0.001 M HCl?
items.rationale: HCl fully dissociates, so [H+] = 1e-3 and pH = 3.

items.stem: A 0.01 M NaOH solution has a pH of:

concepts.base_principle: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]); buffer best near pKa.

items.stem: An acetate buffer (pKa 4.76) contains equal concentrations of acetic acid and acetate. Its pH is approximately:
items.rationale: When [A-] = [HA], log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa.

items.stem: To build a buffer centered at pH 7.4, you should choose a weak acid with a pKa:
items.rationale: Buffering capacity is greatest within about 1 unit of the pKa, so pick pKa near the target pH.

concepts.base_principle: SN1 (tertiary, racemization) vs SN2 (primary, inversion).

items.stem: A tertiary alkyl halide in a polar protic solvent with a weak nucleophile most likely reacts by:
items.rationale: Tertiary substrates stabilize carbocations; polar protic solvent and weak nucleophile favor SN1.

items.stem: An SN2 reaction occurring at a stereocenter produces:
items.rationale: Backside attack in SN2 inverts the stereocenter (Walden inversion).

concepts.base_principle: Catalysts lower Ea but do not change Keq or dG.

items.stem: Adding a catalyst to a reaction already at equilibrium will:

options.C: Not change the position of equilibrium

items.rationale: Catalysts speed forward and reverse rates equally, leaving the equilibrium position and Keq unchanged.

items.stem: An enzyme accelerates a reaction by:
items.rationale: Enzymes lower activation energy; thermodynamics (dG, Keq) are unchanged.

concepts.base_principle: Systems shift to counteract an imposed change.

items.stem: For N2 + 3 H2 <-> 2 NH3, continuously removing NH3 as it forms will:

options.B: Shift the equilibrium right (make more NH3)

items.rationale: Removing a product shifts the equilibrium toward products to replace it.

items.stem: Increasing pressure by decreasing volume on a gas-phase equilibrium shifts it toward:

options.A: The side with more moles of gas
options.B: The side with fewer moles of gas

items.rationale: The system reduces pressure by favoring the side with fewer gas moles.

concepts.base_principle: JND is a constant proportion of the stimulus.

items.stem: You can just detect a 2 g increase added to a 100 g weight. On a 200 g weight, the just-noticeable increase will be about:
items.rationale: Weber's law keeps the ratio constant (2%), so 2% of 200 g is 4 g.

items.stem: Weber's law states that the just-noticeable difference is:

options.B: A constant proportion of the stimulus

items.rationale: The JND scales as a constant fraction of the baseline stimulus intensity.

concepts.base_principle: Criterion shifts trade off hits and false alarms.

items.stem: A radiologist becomes more willing to call ambiguous scans 'positive.' This lowered criterion will increase:
items.rationale: A more liberal criterion increases both true positives (hits) and false positives (false alarms).

items.stem: Reporting that a tumor is present when in fact none is present is a:
items.rationale: Saying 'signal' when there is none is a false alarm.

concepts.base_principle: Working memory holds about 7 plus or minus 2 items; chunking helps.

items.stem: Grouping a 10-digit phone number into three chunks improves recall mainly by:

options.B: Working within the ~7 plus or minus 2 capacity through chunking

items.rationale: Chunking packages items so the limited-capacity store holds more information.

items.stem: Short-term/working memory capacity is best estimated as about:
items.rationale: Miller's classic estimate is about seven plus or minus two items.

concepts.base_principle: Classical pairs stimuli; operant uses consequences.

items.stem: A dog salivates to a bell that was previously paired with food. The bell is the:
items.rationale: After pairing, the formerly neutral bell becomes the conditioned stimulus.

items.stem: Learning that is driven by the consequences (rewards or punishers) following a behavior is:
items.rationale: Operant conditioning changes behavior through its consequences.

concepts.base_principle: Negative reinforcement removes an aversive to increase behavior.

items.stem: A driver buckles the seatbelt to stop an annoying beep, and afterward buckles up more often. This is:
items.rationale: Removing the aversive beep increases the behavior, the definition of negative reinforcement.

items.stem: Taking aspirin removes a headache, making a person more likely to take aspirin in the future. This is an example of:
items.rationale: An aversive state is removed and the behavior increases, so it is negative reinforcement.

concepts.base_principle: Conformity to a unanimous majority; a dissenter reduces it.

items.stem: A participant gives an obviously wrong answer in order to match a unanimous group. This best illustrates:
items.rationale: Matching peers' behavior against one's own judgment is conformity (as in Asch).

items.stem: In Asch-style situations, conformity tends to decrease most when:

options.B: At least one other person dissents

items.rationale: A single dissenting ally sharply reduces conformity pressure.

concepts.base_principle: Overattribute others' behavior to disposition over situation.

items.stem: Seeing a coworker arrive late, you conclude they are lazy rather than considering traffic. This is:
items.rationale: Attributing another's behavior to disposition while ignoring situational causes is the FAE.

items.stem: The fundamental attribution error involves overweighting ___ and underweighting ___ when explaining others' behavior.
items.rationale: It overweights internal disposition and underweights external situation.

concepts.base_principle: Audiences help easy tasks but hurt hard/novel ones.

items.stem: An expert pianist performs a well-rehearsed piece even better in front of an audience. This illustrates:
items.rationale: An audience improves performance on easy, well-learned tasks (social facilitation).

items.stem: A student struggles more on a brand-new, difficult problem when observed than when alone. Social facilitation predicts that an audience will:

options.B: Impair performance on difficult or novel tasks

items.rationale: Arousal from an audience impairs performance on difficult or unmastered tasks.
